basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: al-Najm — “The Star” — is the great sūra of the Ascension (miʿrāj). It opens with an oath and a defence of the Prophet: your companion has not gone astray… he does not speak out of caprice: it is only a revelation. Qushayrī sees in it a unique distinction: God Himself undertakes his defence; how should he speak from passion, he who stands in the place of intimate confidence, wholly ravished? The sūra unfolds the vision of the Presence — the drawing near “at two bow-lengths or closer,” the secret revelation to the servant, the lote-tree of the boundary, the gaze that “neither swerved nor strayed” — in which Qushayrī sees the summit of adab in contemplation. It then denounces the idols (al-Lāt, al-ʿUzzā, Manāt) as mere empty names, and conjecture (ẓann) which avails nothing against the truth. It recalls the truths of the scrolls of Moses and Abraham: no one bears another's burden; man has only the fruit of his own striving; and the litany of “it is He who…” — who makes laugh and weep, makes die and live. It is sealed upon the imminent Hour and the command to prostrate.
themes: the vanity of idols and of conjecture

versets.trad: By the star when it sets!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports the readings: the Pleiades when they decline; the genus of the stars; the stars of the Qurʾān descending upon the heart of the Prophet; or again: an oath by the Prophet himself on his return from the Ascension (miʿrāj). And he adds: it is said: an oath by the light of the hearts of the knowers and the stars of the intellects of the seekers. The oath opens the great sūra of the Ascension.

mots.racine: ن ج م / ه و ي
mots.sens: by the star when it declines
mots.def: najm: star; hawā: to fall, to decline.

versets.trad: Your companion has not gone astray, nor is he in error;
versets.commentaire: The answer to the oath: your companion has not gone astray. Qushayrī: he never departed from tawḥīd, and did not deviate. And he sees in it a distinction: God Himself undertakes the defence of the Prophet against what is imputed to him — unlike Noah, whom He had say “there is no error in me,” or Hūd: “there is no madness in me.” The meaning: his companion has not gone astray, and was not for a single instant distracted from contemplation.

mots.racine: ض ل ل / غ و ي
mots.sens: your companion did not err nor deviate
mots.def: ḍalla: to go astray; ghawā: to deviate.

versets.trad: he does not speak out of caprice.
versets.commentaire: “He does not speak from passion.” Qushayrī sees in this yet another distinction of the Prophet: to David He said “do not follow passion,” but of our Prophet He says “he does not speak from passion.” And how should he speak from passion, he who stands in the place of intimate confidence? Outwardly reined by the bridle of godly fear, inwardly in the sanctuary of the Master, purified of the troubles of humanity, raised to the contemplation of the divine Oneness, unveiled to the majesty of the All-Sufficient, wholly ravished — there remains of him, for the Real by the Real, only a remnant: one who is thus, how should he speak from passion?

mots.racine: ن ط ق / ه و ي
mots.sens: he does not speak according to passion
mots.def: naṭaqa: to speak; hawā: caprice, passion.

versets.trad: It is only a revelation that is revealed,
versets.commentaire: “It is only a revelation inspired to him.” The word of the Prophet is not his own: it is waḥy, an inspiration sent down. What issues from his lips is not human opinion but a transmission from Heaven. The verse seals the defence of the three preceding ones: neither error, nor caprice, but pure reception of the divine Word.

mots.racine: و ح ي
mots.sens: it is only an inspired revelation

versets.trad: taught to him by One mighty in power,
versets.commentaire: “He was taught it by the One mighty in powers” — Gabriel. Qushayrī identifies him with the angel who bears the revelation. The chain of transmission is sacred: the Word comes from God, by the intermediary of the angel “of intense powers,” to the heart of the Prophet. Nothing human is mingled with it.

mots.racine: ع ل م / ش د د / ق و ي
mots.sens: taught him by the one mighty in powers
mots.def: shadīd al-quwā: Gabriel, endowed with intense powers.

versets.trad: possessed of clear discernment. He stood upright in majesty,
versets.commentaire: “Endowed with vigour (dhū mirra), he stood upright” — Gabriel, in his form. Qushayrī: dhū mirra means endowed with strength; it is Gabriel. The angel rises in his true stature, a mighty manifestation of the heavenly mediation. The vision that follows is of this glorious creature, at the highest horizon.

mots.racine: م ر ر / س و ي
mots.sens: endowed with vigour, he stood upright
mots.def: mirra: strength, vigour; istawā: to stand upright.

versets.trad: standing at the highest horizon.
versets.commentaire: “And he was at the highest horizon” — Gabriel, in his first form, at the upper limit of heaven. The scene unfolds at the bounds of the visible: the angel manifests himself there where the world of forms ends and the mystery begins. The Prophet is to contemplate him in his reality.

mots.racine: أ ف ق / ع ل و
mots.sens: he was at the highest horizon

versets.trad: Then He drew near and came down,
themes: the drawing near and the descent
versets.commentaire: “Then he drew near and came closer still.” Qushayrī gives several readings: Gabriel drew near to Muḥammad; or — and this is more profound — Muḥammad drew near to his Lord with a nearness of honour. It is said: Muḥammad drew near to his Lord through the subtleties of knowledge entrusted to him, and came closer through the settling of his heart toward the One who had drawn him near. The mystery of proximity opens.

mots.racine: د ن و / د ل و
mots.sens: then he drew near and came closer
mots.def: danā: to draw near; tadallā: to come closer still.

versets.trad: so that he was at the distance of two bow-lengths, or nearer still,
versets.commentaire: “He was at two bow-lengths, or nearer.” Qushayrī: between him and God, the measure of two bows — a nearness of honour, not of distance. And he recalls the Arab custom: when they wished to seal a covenant, they joined their two bows together — an image of the pact of friendship. Then God corrects: “or nearer” — far closer still. The proximity surpasses all measure.

mots.racine: ق و س / د ن و
mots.sens: he was at two bow-lengths or nearer
mots.def: qāb qawsayn: the length of two bows; adnā: nearer.

versets.trad: and He revealed to His servant what He revealed.
versets.commentaire: “He revealed to His servant what He revealed.” Qushayrī meditates upon the veil of this secret: He entrusted to him charges that none knew; it is said: “Did I not find you an orphan and shelter you? astray and guide you? destitute and enrich you?” Best to say: all that has been advanced is beautiful, and still more that no one has known — all this was for him, that night, alone: He raised him where He raised him, received him as He received him, drew him near where no nearness took place before or after, without anyone knowing what passed between them of the secret.

mots.racine: و ح ي / ع ب د
mots.sens: He revealed to His servant what He revealed

versets.trad: The heart did not lie about what it saw.
versets.commentaire: “The heart did not belie what it saw.” Qushayrī: the heart of Muḥammad did not belie what his eyes saw of the signs. It is said: he saw his Lord that night according to the attribute by which he knew Him before seeing Him. The vision of the Prophet is neither illusion nor falsehood: what the heart (fuʾād) contemplated is certain truth. The debate over the nature of this vision — of the eyes or of the heart — opens the highest pages of Sufism.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب / ف أ د / ر أ ي
mots.sens: the heart did not belie what it saw

versets.trad: Will you then dispute with him about what he sees?
versets.commentaire: “Will you contest with him about what he sees?” Qushayrī: will you dispute with him about what he contemplates? The vision of the Prophet is no matter for debate by those who had no part in it. What he saw at the highest horizon, he alone contemplated; the denial of the absent cannot reach it.

mots.racine: م ر ي / ر أ ي
mots.sens: do you contest with him about what he sees?

versets.trad: He saw him, in truth, at another descent,
versets.commentaire: “He saw him at another descent.” Qushayrī: Gabriel saw God another time, while Muḥammad was near the lote-tree of the boundary. The repetition of the vision confirms its reality: it was no fleeting apparition but a renewed contemplation, at the most sacred place of heaven.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / ن ز ل
mots.sens: he saw him at another descent

versets.trad: by the lote-tree of the boundary,
versets.commentaire: “By the lote-tree of the boundary” (sidrat al-muntahā). Qushayrī: a tree in Paradise, the limit of the angels; it is said: there the spirits of the martyrs come to rest; it is said: there the spirits of creatures come to their end, and none knows what lies beyond save God. The sidrat al-muntahā marks the ultimate frontier of the created: beyond it, the pure divine mystery.

mots.racine: س د ر / ن ه ي
mots.sens: by the lote-tree of the boundary
mots.def: sidrat al-muntahā: the lote-tree of the utmost boundary.

versets.trad: there where lies the Garden of the Abode,
versets.commentaire: “Near it is the Garden of the Abode” (jannat al-maʾwā). Qushayrī: a garden among the gardens. The place of the supreme vision is also the place of the abode of the elect: at the frontier of the mystery stands the Garden of the dwelling, there where contemplation reaches its term.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / أ و ي
mots.sens: near it the Garden of the Abode
mots.def: jannat al-maʾwā: the garden of the dwelling.

versets.trad: when the lote-tree was enveloped by that which covers it.
versets.commentaire: “When there covered the lote-tree what covered it.” Qushayrī reports: there covers it what covers it of the angels, which God alone knows; in one account: a fluttering of green birds; it is said: butterflies of gold. The indefinite “what covers it” bespeaks the ineffable: what envelops the sacred place surpasses all description, veiled in light.

mots.racine: غ ش و / س د ر
mots.sens: when there covered the lote-tree what covered it

versets.trad: His gaze did not swerve, nor did it stray.
versets.commentaire: “The gaze neither swerved nor overstepped.” Qushayrī: he did not turn his gaze from what he was permitted to contemplate, nor did he overstep his limit: he kept the rules of adab in the Presence. He cites Abū Yazīd: the Prophet kept his gaze in the Ascension — he neither turned aside nor went beyond — through knowledge of what he was worthy of; he contemplated nothing else, saw no other; then, brought back to the place of instruction, he looked upon Paradise and Hell so as to give tidings of them. The station of election, then that of the common folk.

mots.racine: ز ي غ / ب ص ر / ط غ ي
mots.sens: the gaze neither swerved nor overstepped
mots.def: zāgha: to swerve; ṭaghā: to overstep.

versets.ar: لَقَدْ رَأَىٰ مِنْ آيَاتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرَىٰ
versets.tr: laqad raʾā min āyāti rabbihi l-kubrā
versets.trad: He saw, in truth, the greatest of the signs of his Lord.
versets.commentaire: “He saw, of the signs of his Lord, the greatest.” Qushayrī: it is the supreme Sign that he saw that night; it is said: it is his abiding in the state of meeting his Lord, in the state of wakefulness (ṣaḥw), kept until he beheld Him. The greatest sign is not only what he saw, but the very fact of remaining conscious, steadfast, in contemplation — there where Moses had fallen thunderstruck.

mots.ar: لَقَدْ رَأَىٰ مِنْ آيَاتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرَىٰ
mots.tr: laqad raʾā min āyāti rabbihi l-kubrā
mots.racine: ر أ ي / أ ي ي / ك ب ر
mots.sens: he saw the greatest of the signs of his Lord

versets.trad: Have you considered al-Lāt and al-ʿUzzā,
versets.commentaire: After the supreme vision, the contrast: the idols. Qushayrī: al-Lāt, the idol of Thaqīf; al-ʿUzzā, a tree of Ghaṭafān. The meaning: do these idols you worship have the power to do, for whoever takes refuge in them, what We have done for Muḥammad — the ranks and the election? Against the summit of contemplation stands the vanity of fabricated gods.

mots.racine: ر أ ي
mots.sens: have you considered al-Lāt and al-ʿUzzā
mots.def: al-Lāt, al-ʿUzzā: idols of the Arabs.

versets.trad: and Manāt, the third, the other?
versets.commentaire: “And Manāt, the third, the other” — a rock worshipped by Hudhayl and Khuzāʿa. Three idols, all feminine, which the pagans claimed to be “daughters of God.” The following verse will denounce the incoherence: they attribute daughters to God, while despising daughters for themselves.

mots.sens: and Manāt, the third, the other
mots.def: Manāt: an idol, a worshipped rock.

versets.trad: Shall the male be yours, and the female His?
versets.commentaire: “Is the male for you and the female for Him?” Qushayrī: here they are, these idols all feminine, which they claimed to be daughters of God and angels. How do you choose sons for yourselves and ascribe daughters to God? The absurdity of their theology bursts forth: they lend to God what they themselves disdain. The division they imagine condemns them by its own incoherence.

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / أ ن ث
mots.sens: is the male for you and the female for Him?

versets.trad: That would be an unfair division!
versets.commentaire: “That would then be an unjust division” (qisma ḍīzā). Qushayrī: a defective division (nāqiṣa). The word ḍīzā denotes the lopsided, unjust apportionment: reserving the best (sons) for oneself and assigning to God the lesser (according to their own contempt). The incoherence turns into a condemnation: their theology is inequitable even in its terms.

mots.racine: ق س م / ض ي ز
mots.sens: that is an unjust division

versets.ar: إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا أَسْمَاءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوهَا أَنتُمْ وَآبَاؤُكُم مَّا أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَانٍ ۚ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَمَا تَهْوَى الْأَنفُسُ ۖ وَلَقَدْ جَاءَهُم مِّن رَّبِّهِمُ الْهُدَىٰ
versets.tr: in hiya illā asmāʾun sammaytumūhā antum wa-ābāʾukum mā anzala llāhu bihā min sulṭānin in yattabiʿūna illā ẓ-ẓanna wa-mā tahwā l-anfusu wa-laqad jāʾahum min rabbihimu l-hudā
versets.trad: These are but names that you and your fathers have given them, for which God has sent down no authority. They follow only conjecture and what their souls desire, although the Guidance of their Lord has come to them.
versets.commentaire: “These are but names you have forged.” Qushayrī: you invented these names without God having commanded or permitted you to; you follow conjecture (ẓann), and conjecture avails nothing against the truth. And he transposes it to the Way: just as the conjecture of the deniers earned them ignorance and error, so on this Way, whoever clings to the attributes of conjecture attains nothing of the Reality. Only certainty (taḥaqquq) gives access to the true.

mots.racine: س م و
mots.sens: these are but names you have given

mots.ar: إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَمَا تَهْوَى الْأَنفُسُ
mots.tr: in yattabiʿūna illā ẓ-ẓanna wa-mā tahwā l-anfus
mots.racine: ت ب ع / ظ ن ن / ه و ي
mots.sens: they follow only conjecture and passion

versets.trad: Shall man obtain whatever he desires?
versets.commentaire: “Shall man have whatever he covets?” Qushayrī: no, man does not obtain all that he desires: he wishes long life, ease, endless abundance — and none attains it fully. And, higher: to wish that one's own will should prevail in all things is not an attribute of the creature, but of God, to whom belongs whatever He wills. Human desire is measured; only the will of God is fulfilled without limit.

mots.racine: م ن ي
mots.sens: shall man have what he desires?

versets.trad: To God belong the Hereafter and the former life.
themes: to God belong the two worlds
versets.commentaire: “To God belong the Hereafter and the here-below.” Qushayrī: His by creation and by possession; He is the Sovereign Owner, holder of perfect kingship; as for the created, want clings to it in everything. The two worlds are in His hands: whoever desires that his own will should prevail forgets that all sovereignty belongs to the King alone.

mots.racine: أ خ ر / أ و ل
mots.sens: to God belong the Hereafter and the here-below
mots.def: ākhira: the Hereafter; ūlā: the here-below.

versets.ar: وَكَم مِّن مَّلَكٍ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ لَا تُغْنِي شَفَاعَتُهُمْ شَيْئًا إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِ أَن يَأْذَنَ اللَّهُ لِمَن يَشَاءُ وَيَرْضَىٰ
versets.tr: wa-kam min malakin fī s-samāwāti lā tughnī shafāʿatuhum shayʾan illā min baʿdi an yaʾdhana llāhu li-man yashāʾu wa-yarḍā
versets.trad: How many an angel is there in the heavens whose intercession will avail nothing, save after God has permitted it, for whom He wills and with whom He is pleased.
versets.commentaire: “How many an angel whose intercession avails nothing, save after God has permitted it.” Qushayrī: a refutation of those who said: the angels are our intercessors with God. If the intercession of the angels, despite their number and their nearness, is worth nothing save with the divine permission — what then are the idols worth? There is no intercession without the authorization and good pleasure of God.

mots.racine: غ ن ي / ش ف ع
mots.sens: their intercession avails nothing

mots.ar: إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِ أَن يَأْذَنَ اللَّهُ لِمَن يَشَاءُ وَيَرْضَىٰ
mots.tr: illā min baʿdi an yaʾdhana llāhu li-man yashāʾu wa-yarḍā
mots.racine: أ ذ ن / ر ض و
mots.sens: save after God permits, for whom He is pleased
mots.def: adhina: to permit; raḍiya: to be pleased.

versets.ar: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ لَيُسَمُّونَ الْمَلَائِكَةَ تَسْمِيَةَ الْأُنثَىٰ
versets.tr: inna lladhīna lā yuʾminūna bi-l-ākhirati la-yusammūna l-malāʾikata tasmiyata l-unthā
versets.trad: Those who do not believe in the Hereafter give the angels female names,
themes: the female names of the angels
versets.commentaire: “Those who do not believe in the Hereafter give the angels the names of females.” The error of the pagans: making the angels “daughters of God.” The denial of the Hereafter goes hand in hand with this mythological fabrication: whoever does not believe in the reckoning invents intermediaries as he pleases. The word that follows lays bare its root: pure conjecture.

mots.racine: س م و / م ل ك / أ ن ث
mots.sens: they name the angels with female names

versets.ar: وَمَا لَهُم بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ ۖ وَإِنَّ الظَّنَّ لَا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئًا
versets.tr: wa-mā lahum bihi min ʿilmin in yattabiʿūna illā ẓ-ẓanna wa-inna ẓ-ẓanna lā yughnī mina l-ḥaqqi shayʾan
versets.trad: though they have no knowledge of it. They follow only conjecture, but conjecture avails nothing against the Truth.
versets.commentaire: “They have no knowledge of it: they follow only conjecture, and conjecture avails nothing against the truth.” A cardinal verse, repeated (cf. v. 23). Qushayrī applies it to the Way: conjecture (ẓann) gives access to no reality. Only certainty (yaqīn) counts. He distinguishes, however: fair thinking toward God (ẓann jamīl) is not of this register — what is at issue here is the conjecture that claims to know without knowing.

mots.racine: ع ل م
mots.sens: they have no knowledge of it

mots.ar: وَإِنَّ الظَّنَّ لَا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئًا
mots.tr: wa-inna ẓ-ẓanna lā yughnī mina l-ḥaqqi shayʾan
mots.racine: ظ ن ن / غ ن ي / ح ق ق
mots.sens: conjecture avails nothing against the truth

versets.ar: فَأَعْرِضْ عَن مَّن تَوَلَّىٰ عَن ذِكْرِنَا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَيَاةَ الدُّنْيَا
versets.tr: fa-aʿriḍ ʿan man tawallā ʿan dhikrinā wa-lam yurid illā l-ḥayāta d-dunyā
versets.trad: So turn away from him who turns his back on Our remembrance and desires nothing but the life of this world.
versets.commentaire: “Turn away from whoever turns from Our remembrance and wants only the life of here-below.” Qushayrī: withdraw from whoever turns from the Qurʾān and from faith, refuses to ponder its meanings, and desires only this world. Whoever aspires only to the transient closes himself to the Reminder; to turn away from him is not harshness but recognition of his choice. The heart turned toward the lower world has no ear left for Heaven.

mots.ar: فَأَعْرِضْ عَن مَّن تَوَلَّىٰ عَن ذِكْرِنَا
mots.tr: fa-aʿriḍ ʿan man tawallā ʿan dhikrinā
mots.racine: ع ر ض / و ل ي / ذ ك ر
mots.sens: turn away from him who turns from Our remembrance
mots.def: aʿraḍa: to turn away; dhikr: remembrance.

mots.racine: ر و د / ح ي ي
mots.sens: and wants only the life of here-below

versets.ar: ذَٰلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُم مِّنَ الْعِلْمِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ اهْتَدَىٰ
versets.tr: dhālika mablaghuhum mina l-ʿilmi inna rabbaka huwa aʿlamu bi-man ḍalla ʿan sabīlihi wa-huwa aʿlamu bi-mani htadā
versets.trad: That is the sum of their knowledge! Truly your Lord knows best who has strayed from His path, and He knows best who is rightly guided.
versets.commentaire: “That is the sum of their knowledge.” Qushayrī: they were content with the here-below only because they did not know the account of the Hereafter. Their knowledge stops at the visible: desiring only this world, they know no other. And God, for His part, knows perfectly the strayed and the guided: the knowledge that confines man to the transient is the very opposite of the Knowledge that embraces both worlds.

mots.racine: ب ل غ / ع ل م
mots.sens: that is the sum of their knowledge

mots.ar: هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِ
mots.tr: huwa aʿlamu bi-man ḍalla ʿan sabīlih
mots.racine: ع ل م / ض ل ل
mots.sens: He knows best who strays from His path

versets.ar: وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا بِمَا عَمِلُوا وَيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا بِالْحُسْنَى
versets.tr: wa-lillāhi mā fī s-samāwāti wa-mā fī l-arḍi li-yajziya lladhīna asāʾū bimā ʿamilū wa-yajziya lladhīna aḥsanū bi-l-ḥusnā
versets.trad: To God belongs all that is in the heavens and on the earth, that He may requite those who do evil for what they have done and reward those who do good with the fairest reward.
versets.commentaire: “To God belongs all that is in the heavens and on the earth, that He may requite the evildoers according to their deeds and reward the doers of good with the fairest of rewards.” Universal sovereignty has an end: justice. He who owns everything requites each according to his work — sanction for evil, beauty (ḥusnā) for good. The divine possession is not indifference but equity.

mots.racine: ج ز ي / س و أ / ع م ل
mots.sens: that He requite the evildoers according to their deeds

mots.racine: ج ز ي / ح س ن
mots.sens: and reward the doers of good with the fairest
mots.def: aḥsana: to do good; ḥusnā: the fairest.

versets.ar: الَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبَائِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَالْفَوَاحِشَ إِلَّا اللَّمَمَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ وَاسِعُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ ۚ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِذْ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ الْأَرْضِ وَإِذْ أَنتُمْ أَجِنَّةٌ فِي بُطُونِ أُمَّهَاتِكُمْ ۖ فَلَا تُزَكُّوا أَنفُسَكُمْ ۖ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ اتَّقَىٰ
versets.tr: alladhīna yajtanibūna kabāʾira l-ithmi wa-l-fawāḥisha illā l-lamama inna rabbaka wāsiʿu l-maghfirati huwa aʿlamu bikum idh anshaʾakum mina l-arḍi wa-idh antum ajinnatun fī buṭūni ummahātikum fa-lā tuzakkū anfusakum huwa aʿlamu bi-mani ttaqā
versets.trad: To those who shun the major sins and indecencies, committing only slight faults, your Lord grants His pardon abundantly. He knows you best from the moment He brought you forth from the earth and when you were still embryos in your mothers' wombs. So do not claim purity for yourselves; God knows best who fears Him.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī dwells on “do not claim purity for yourselves” (fa-lā tuzakkū anfusakum): to boast of one's purity is a sign that man is veiled far from God, for whoever is drawn to the very end and absorbed in the contemplation of his Lord does not claim to be pure. And he adds: whoever believes there is worse than himself on earth is proud; the Muslim must see every Muslim as better than himself — the old man more obedient, the young man less burdened with sins. God alone knows who fears Him (taqwā): self-justification is the opposite of true fear.

mots.racine: ج ن ب / أ ث م / ف ح ش
mots.sens: who shun the major sins and indecencies

mots.racine: ز ك و / ن ف س
mots.sens: do not claim purity for yourselves
mots.def: zakkā: to declare pure, to justify.

versets.trad: Have you seen the one who turned his back,
versets.commentaire: “Have you seen the one who turned away?” Qushayrī: he turned away from the Real. The verse opens the portrait of the miser who has turned his back on generosity and faith. Turning away (tawallī) is the gesture opposed to the “flight toward God”: it is fleeing far from the source.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / و ل ي
mots.sens: have you seen the one who turned away

versets.trad: who gave a little, then withheld?
versets.commentaire: “He gave little, then stopped” (akdā). Qushayrī: he gave a little in alms, then cut off his giving. The miser gives a crumb, then runs dry. The verb akdā evokes the well whose water dries up: the surface generosity that freezes reveals a heart that turns away. It is, says Qushayrī, the hypocrite who helps a little in the fight, then stops.

mots.racine: ع ط و / ك د ي
mots.sens: he gave little and ran dry
mots.def: aʿṭā: to give; akdā: to cut off, to run dry.

versets.trad: Does he possess the knowledge of the unseen, so that he sees?
themes: the supposed knowledge of the hidden
versets.commentaire: “Does he hold the knowledge of the Unseen, that he sees?” Qushayrī: does he see his lot in the Hereafter? The miser who holds back his gift acts as though he knew the invisible — as though he had the assurance that nothing would be asked of him. But none knows the ghayb: his stinginess rests on an illusion of knowledge.

mots.racine: ع ل م / غ ي ب / ر أ ي
mots.sens: does he hold the knowledge of the Unseen, that he sees?

versets.ar: أَمْ لَمْ يُنَبَّأْ بِمَا فِي صُحُفِ مُوسَىٰ
versets.tr: am lam yunabbaʾ bimā fī ṣuḥufi mūsā
versets.trad: Has he not been informed of what is in the scrolls of Moses,
versets.commentaire: “Has he not been informed of what is in the scrolls of Moses?” The verse introduces the founding truths, common to the ancient Scriptures. The denier cannot plead ignorance: the wisdom of the earlier prophets has already proclaimed the laws of responsibility that the following verses will recall.

mots.ar: أَمْ لَمْ يُنَبَّأْ بِمَا فِي صُحُفِ مُوسَىٰ
mots.tr: am lam yunabbaʾ bimā fī ṣuḥufi mūsā
mots.racine: ن ب أ / ص ح ف
mots.sens: has he not been informed of the scrolls of Moses?

versets.trad: and of Abraham, the ever faithful?
versets.commentaire: “And of Abraham who fulfilled his charge (waffā).” Qushayrī: he carried out to the very end what was asked of him, in his person, his goods, and his son. Abraham is the model of perfect fidelity: the trial of the sacrifice, of the fire, of exile — he “paid” everything. The scrolls of Abraham proclaim the truths that the following verse unfolds.

mots.racine: و ف ي
mots.sens: and Abraham who fulfilled his charge
mots.def: waffā: to fulfil completely, to pay in full.

versets.trad: namely this: that no bearer of burdens shall bear the burden of another,
versets.commentaire: The first truth of the scrolls: “no bearer bears the burden of another.” Each is answerable for his own deeds alone. This law cuts clean: neither idols nor ancestors will bear anyone's weight, and none will be punished for another's fault. Responsibility is strictly personal.

mots.racine: و ز ر
mots.sens: that no bearer bears the burden of another
mots.def: wizr: burden; wāzira: a laden soul.

versets.ar: وَأَن لَّيْسَ لِلْإِنسَانِ إِلَّا مَا سَعَىٰ
versets.tr: wa-an laysa lil-insāni illā mā saʿā
versets.trad: and that man shall have only what he has striven for;
versets.commentaire: “Man has only that toward which he has striven (mā saʿā).” Qushayrī unfolds the degrees of striving: whoever toils for the here-below loses his bargain in it; whoever toils for Paradise gains it; whoever toils to master his soul attains the good pleasure of God; whoever toils in the quest (irāda), God accepts his effort and then guides him to Himself; and the sinner, if he toils to seek pardon with a contrite heart, will find the reward, the nearness, the honour. No sincere effort is lost.

mots.ar: وَأَن لَّيْسَ لِلْإِنسَانِ إِلَّا مَا سَعَىٰ
mots.tr: wa-an laysa lil-insāni illā mā saʿā
mots.racine: س ع ي
mots.sens: man has only that toward which he strove

versets.trad: that his striving shall in time be seen,
versets.commentaire: “His striving shall soon be seen.” Qushayrī: whoever counts his breaths with God, without slackening or failing, will see the fruit of his effort acknowledged (mashkūr) in this world and in the Hereafter. Nothing of sincere toil is lost: it will be made manifest, brought to light on the Day when all appears. The hidden striving of the heart finds its recognition.

mots.racine: س ع ي / ر أ ي
mots.sens: his striving shall soon be seen
mots.def: saʿy: striving; ruʾiya: to be seen.

versets.trad: and that he shall then be repaid with the fullest of rewards;
versets.commentaire: “Then he shall be requited with the fullest requital (al-jazāʾ al-awfā).” Qushayrī: it is the greatest reward, the most sublime, without cutting off or refusal. God requites not by strict measure but beyond: sincere effort receives an overflowing reward. And he adds: He honours him by addressing him His word without intermediary: “My servant, your striving is acknowledged, your sin forgiven.”

mots.racine: ج ز ي / و ف ي
mots.sens: he shall be requited with the fullest reward
mots.def: jazāʾ: requital; awfā: the most complete.

versets.trad: that to your Lord belongs the final end;
versets.commentaire: “To your Lord is the end (al-muntahā).” Qushayrī: to Him the return and the becoming: things begin from God by creation and come to their end in God by return. It is said: when discourse reaches God, fall silent. It is said: when the servant attains the knowledge of God, there is nothing beyond — save the graces God causes to flow upon him. Everything ends in God: He is the origin and the term.

mots.racine: ن ه ي
mots.sens: to your Lord is the end

versets.trad: that it is He who makes laugh and makes weep;
versets.commentaire: “It is He who makes laugh and makes weep.” Qushayrī multiplies the meanings: He makes the earth laugh with vegetation and the sky weep with rain; He makes the believer laugh in the Hereafter and weep in this world; He makes the hearts of the knowers laugh with good pleasure (riḍā) and their eyes weep with the fear of separation; He makes laugh by His mercy and makes the enemies weep by His wrath. Laughter and tears are not of man but of God who arouses them.

mots.racine: ض ح ك / ب ك ي
mots.sens: it is He who makes laugh and weep
mots.def: aḍḥaka: to make laugh; abkā: to make weep.

versets.trad: that it is He who makes die and makes live;
versets.commentaire: “It is He who makes die and makes live.” Qushayrī unfolds the registers: He makes die in the here-below and live again in the grave; He makes the souls of the renunciants die through striving (mujāhada) and the hearts of the knowers live through contemplation (mushāhada); He makes die through works and live through unions; through awe and through intimacy; through occultation and through unveiling; by turning away and by turning toward them. Spiritual death and life are in His hands.

mots.racine: م و ت / ح ي ي
mots.sens: it is He who makes die and live
mots.def: amāta: to make die; aḥyā: to make live.

versets.trad: that it is He who creates the pair, male and female,
versets.commentaire: “It is He who created the pair, the male and the female.” Qushayrī: He named them a pair (zawjayn) because of their pairing at their creation from the drop. A direct refutation of the pagans who ascribed daughters to God: it is He who creates the two sexes, from a single seed. The duality of the sexes is His work, not a human projection upon Him.

mots.racine: خ ل ق / ز و ج / ذ ك ر / أ ن ث
mots.sens: it is He who creates the pair, male and female

versets.trad: from a drop of seed when it is emitted;
versets.commentaire: “From a drop (nuṭfa) when it is emitted.” From this tiny drop, God draws the male and the female. The verse continues the refutation: the One who creates the diversity of the sexes from a single seed has no need of “children.” Meditation on the human origin — the drop — reveals the power of the Creator and the absurdity of ascribing offspring to Him.

mots.racine: ن ط ف / م ن ي
mots.sens: from a drop when it is emitted
mots.def: nuṭfa: drop of seed; tumnā: to be emitted.

versets.trad: that upon Him rests the second creation;
versets.commentaire: “Upon Him rests the second creation (al-nashʾa al-ukhrā).” The One who created a first time will create anew: the resurrection is His charge. The first birth, already accomplished before our eyes, guarantees the second. To deny the return is to deny the very logic of the creation from which one is oneself sprung.

mots.racine: ن ش أ
mots.sens: upon Him rests the second creation

versets.trad: that it is He, in truth, who grants riches and contentment;
versets.commentaire: “It is He who enriches (aghnā) and provides goods (aqnā).” Qushayrī gives several readings: aghnā: He gave wealth; aqnā: He increased possessions; or: He made one needy — so He created riches and poverty; it is said: aqnā means “He contented with what He gave”; it is said: aghnā, He made satisfied; aqnā, He made well-pleased. Beyond material wealth, the true gift is the contentment (qināʿa) of the heart.

mots.racine: غ ن ي / ق ن و
mots.sens: it is He who enriches and contents
mots.def: aghnā: to enrich; aqnā: to provide, to content.

versets.trad: that He is the Lord of Sirius;
versets.commentaire: “And it is He, the Lord of Sirius (al-Shiʿrā).” Qushayrī: Sirius is a star that rises after Gemini, at the height of the dog days; Khuzāʿa worshipped it — God makes known that He is the Lord of the object of their worship. The star the pagans deified is but a creature of which God is the Master. At the start of the sūra, one swore “by the star”; here, God is the very Lord of the worshipped star.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ش ع ر
mots.sens: it is He, the Lord of Sirius

versets.trad: that He destroyed the ancient ʿĀd,
versets.commentaire: “It is He who annihilated the ancient ʿĀd” — the people of Hūd. Qushayrī distinguishes: ʿĀd the first, that is the people of Hūd; ʿĀd the second, that is Iram of the pillars. The Lord of the star (verse 49) is also the Master of history: He who creates the stars annihilates the rebellious peoples. The litany of “it is He who…” embraces cosmos and history.

mots.racine: ه ل ك / أ و ل
mots.sens: it is He who destroyed the first ʿĀd

versets.trad: and the Thamūd, leaving none,
versets.commentaire: “And the Thamūd — He left none of them.” The people of Ṣāliḥ were wholly effaced. The formula “fa-mā abqā” (He left none to remain) underscores the totality of the annihilation. None survives of those who rise against the divine order: the litany of the chastised peoples recalls the constant law of justice.

mots.racine: ب ق ي
mots.sens: and the Thamūd, He left none of them

versets.ar: وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ مِّن قَبْلُ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا هُمْ أَظْلَمَ وَأَطْغَىٰ
versets.tr: wa-qawma nūḥin min qablu innahum kānū hum aẓlama wa-aṭghā
versets.trad: as also, before them, the people of Noah, who were among the most iniquitous and the most rebellious.
versets.commentaire: “And the people of Noah before them: they were more unjust and more rebellious.” Qushayrī: they were more unjust than the others and more deviant, because of the length of their lives and the strength of their bodies. The longer the reprieve, the worse the injustice grew: their longevity was no mercy received but excess accumulated.

mots.sens: and the people of Noah before them
mots.def: qawm Nūḥ: the people of Noah.

mots.racine: ظ ل م / ط غ ي
mots.sens: they were more unjust and more rebellious
mots.def: aẓlam: more unjust; aṭghā: more rebellious.

versets.trad: And the overturned city, it is He who cast it down,
versets.commentaire: “And the overturned city (al-muʾtafika), He cast it down.” Qushayrī: these are the cities of the people of Lot; Gabriel turned them over upon them; they were overturned, top to bottom. The verb ahwā denotes the fall: God tore them from their foundation, turned them over, and hurled them to the ground. The city of vice is literally overturned by the decree.

mots.racine: أ ف ك / ه و ي
mots.sens: the overturned city, He cast it down
mots.def: muʾtafika: overturned city; ahwā: to cast down.

versets.trad: that He covered with what covers it.
versets.commentaire: “And there covered it what covered it.” The indefinite bespeaks the unspeakable of the chastisement: the rain of stones, the total upheaval. As “that which covers the lote-tree” bespoke the ineffable of grace (verse 16), “that which covered the city” bespeaks the ineffable of the chastisement. The two extremes — the light and the ruin — escape description.

mots.racine: غ ش و
mots.sens: there covered it what covered it

versets.trad: Which of your Lord's bounties, then, would you dispute?
versets.commentaire: “About which of the bounties of your Lord do you doubt?” Qushayrī: about which bounty of your Lord, O man, do you doubt? — and this after the enumeration of His favours and His kindnesses. The litany of “it is He who…” closes with this address: before so many signs, from the cosmos to history, doubt has no excuse left. To acknowledge the bounties is to come out of conjecture.

mots.racine: أ ل و / ر ب ب / م ر ي
mots.sens: about which of your Lord's bounties do you doubt?

versets.trad: This is a warner of the line of those of old.
versets.commentaire: “This is a warner of the kind of the warners of old.” Qushayrī: it is Muḥammad, whom We have sent as a warner just as We sent the other messengers. The Prophet stands within the chain of the prophets: his message is not novelty but continuation. The truths of the scrolls of Moses and Abraham, he bears them in his turn.

mots.racine: ن ذ ر / أ و ل
mots.sens: this is a warner of the warners of old

versets.trad: The Imminent draws near.
versets.commentaire: “The Imminent has drawn near” (azifat al-āzifa). Qushayrī: the Resurrection is near. And he transposes: when the resurrection of this community arises — today —, none unveils it but He; and their resurrection takes place more than once a day: it rises through separation, through distancing, through the tearing apart. The cosmic Hour has its echo in the heart of the lover, who dies and rises again at every separation.

mots.racine: أ ز ف
mots.sens: the Imminent has drawn near
mots.def: azifa: to be imminent; āzifa: the near Hour.

versets.ar: لَيْسَ لَهَا مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ كَاشِفَةٌ
versets.tr: laysa lahā min dūni llāhi kāshifatun
versets.trad: None but God can unveil its coming.
versets.commentaire: “None but God can unveil it.” Qushayrī: none can bring it about save God, and when He has raised it, none can repel it or lift it save God. And he joins to it the inner resurrection: when the resurrection of the lovers arises — through abandonment, distancing, separation —, none but He is its unveiler. God alone holds the Hour, the visible and the hidden.

mots.ar: لَيْسَ لَهَا مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ كَاشِفَةٌ
mots.tr: laysa lahā min dūni llāhi kāshifa
mots.racine: ك ش ف
mots.sens: none but God unveils it
mots.def: kāshifa: one who unveils, who removes.

versets.trad: Do you then marvel at this discourse,
versets.commentaire: “Is it at this Discourse that you marvel?” Qushayrī: is it at this Qurʾān that you marvel, and are in doubt, and at which you scoff? Marvelling at the Qurʾān is not admiration but mocking disbelief. After the evocation of the Hour, the verse turns back to the hearers: their astonishment betrays their refusal.

mots.racine: ح د ث / ع ج ب
mots.sens: is it at this Discourse that you marvel?

versets.trad: and laugh, and do not weep,
versets.commentaire: “You laugh and do not weep.” In verse 43, it is God who “makes laugh and makes weep”; here, the deniers laugh when they ought to weep. Laughter before the warning is the mark of an unfeeling heart: before the Hour that approaches, tears would be the just response, but they know only how to scoff.

mots.racine: ض ح ك / ب ك ي
mots.sens: you laugh and do not weep
mots.def: ḍaḥika: to laugh; bakā: to weep.

versets.trad: while you remain heedless?
versets.commentaire: “And you are heedless (sāmidūn).” Qushayrī: distracted, negligent (lāhūn). Sumūd is frivolous absorption, the head held high in inadvertence. Instead of humbling themselves before the Hour, they remain frozen in their distraction. The final verse will shatter this heedlessness with the call to prostration.

mots.racine: س م د
mots.sens: and you are heedless
mots.def: sāmid: distracted, frivolous, head held high.

versets.trad: So prostrate yourselves before God, and worship Him...
versets.commentaire: The sūra closes upon the command to prostrate (a verse of sajda): “Prostrate yourselves before God and worship Him!” Qushayrī: prostrate yourselves to God and worship none other than Him. After the denial, the laughter, and the heedlessness, the one just response: to fall face to the ground before the One. The sūra, opened upon the Prophet's contemplation at the highest horizon, ends upon the invitation given to all to prostrate.

mots.racine: س ج د / ع ب د
mots.sens: prostrate yourselves before God and worship Him
mots.def: sajada: to prostrate; ʿabada: to worship.
